title: Sudoku Solver
problemStatement:
Write a program to solve a Sudoku puzzle by filling the empty cells. A Sudoku solution must satisfy all of the following rules:

  • Each of the digits 1-9 must occur exactly once in each row.
  • Each of the digits 1-9 must occur exactly once in each column.
  • Each of the digits 1-9 must occur exactly once in each of the 9 3x3 sub-boxes of the grid.

The '.' character indicates empty cells.

examples.explanation: The given Sudoku board is solved such that all the rules are satisfied.

constraints: The Sudoku board is a 9x9 grid.
The board contains digits '1'-'9' and the character '.'.

solutions.sql:
-- Complete SQL solution
SELECT * FROM table WHERE condition

hints: Consider using backtracking to try possible solutions.
Validate each row, column, and 3x3 sub-box after placing a number.
Prune possibilities early by checking constraints frequently.

editorial.approach: Use backtracking to fill each empty cell with a valid number. For each empty position, try all numbers from 1 to 9 and recursively solve the rest of the board. If a number is not valid, backtrack and try the next number.
editorial.timeComplexity: O(9^(n*n)) where n is the size of the board
editorial.explanation: The solution involves filling each cell with numbers 1 to 9 while checking the constraints. If a number is placed incorrectly, backtracking will revert the change and try the next number.

title: Unique Paths II
problemStatement: You are given an m x n integer array grid. There is a robot initially located at the top-left corner (i.e., grid[0][0]). The robot tries to move to the bottom-right corner (i.e., grid[m - 1][n - 1]). The robot can only move either down or right at any point in time. An obstacle and space are marked as 1 or 0 respectively in grid. A path that the robot takes cannot include any square that is an obstacle. Return the number of possible unique paths that the robot can take to reach the bottom-right corner. The testcases are generated so that the answer will be less than or equal to 2 * 10^9.

examples.explanation: There is one obstacle in the grid. There are two ways to reach the bottom-right corner: 1. Right -> Right -> Down -> Down 2. Down -> Down -> Right -> Right.
There is one obstacle blocking the first row, so the only path is to go down then right.

solutions.sql:
-- Complete SQL solution
SELECT * FROM paths WHERE condition

hints: Consider using dynamic programming to store results of subproblems.
Handle the base cases where the starting or ending point is blocked.
Think about the possibilities to arrive to a cell from its left or top.

editorial.approach: We use dynamic programming to solve this problem. We create a dp table to store the number of ways to reach each cell. Initialize dp[0][0] to 1 if grid[0][0] is not an obstacle. For each cell, if it is not an obstacle, we update its dp value as the sum of the ways to reach from the top and left cells. Finally, return dp[m-1][n-1] as the result.
editorial.explanation: The approach efficiently fills the dp table by iterating through all cells once, utilizing previously computed results to build up the solution.
